Ok, don't mean to get super pedantic here, but when you're synthesizing a single period and repeating it, the effective frequency of your signal is `fs/int(fs/frequency)`.  For low frequencies, this isn't too different from `frequency`; for high frequencies it can be very different.  For example, for MIDI note 90 (~1480 hz) with `fs = 22050`, `fs/int(fs/frequency)` is 1575 Hz.  That's why I synthesized ten periods; this was a quick hack to make the effective frequency `10*fs/int(10*fs/frequency)` which for MIDI note 90 results in about 1490 Hz.  It seemed like a good trade-off as an alternative to adding some logic to keep track of the sample error when you're repeating `short_signal`.  I'm open to ideas if you have a better one (one would be to use a very-fast-to-compute pure Python triangle wave implementation or something, and don't do any repeat tricks).  For reference, here are plots of the semitone error of all MIDI notes from 0 to 110 (assuming notes above 110 are a wash) for repeating a single period vs. repeating 10 periods:
```Python
fs = 22050.
def plot(errs):
    plt.figure(figsize=(15, 8))
    plt.xticks(np.arange(0, 128, 4))
    plt.plot(errs)
    plt.grid()
    plt.xlabel('MIDI Note')
    plt.ylabel('Semitone error')
plot([librosa.hz_to_midi(fs/int(fs/librosa.midi_to_hz(f))) - f
      for f in np.arange(0, 110)])
plt.title('1 period')
plot([librosa.hz_to_midi(10*fs/int(10*fs/librosa.midi_to_hz(f))) - f
      for f in np.arange(0, 110)])
plt.title('10 periods')
```

hey @lasers, thanks for pinging me 🙂 I tested all patches, they work well, except this change above introduces a small regression: `hide_if_disconnected` is not working anymore.

I use the following configuration:

```
xrandr {
  force_on_start = 'DP-1-2'
  output_combinations = 'DP-1-2|eDP-1'
  hide_if_single_combination = true
}

xrandr_rotate {
  screen = 'DP-1-2'
  hide_if_disconnected = true
}
```

What it does is the following: I often have my docking station plugged in the laptop, which in turn makes my external monitor often connected. Sometimes I use only laptop's screen (`eDP-1`), and sometimes I use only external screen (`DP-1-2`), but never both at the same time.

Only my external screen is rotatable, so I don't even want to see `xrandr_rotate` as a module when I'm currently using laptop's screen.

Now, this line above before was previously discovering only that screen that is currently showing output, not all connected screens, and that made a difference below in computing `selected_screen_disconnected`. The new code now returns both screens always, regardless of which one is currently showing output and which one is not outputting anything.

Output of `xrandr` when both screens are connected, but ...:
- only `DP-1-2` is used:
    ```
    Screen 0: minimum 320 x 200, current 3840 x 2160, maximum 8192 x 8192
    eDP-1 connected primary (normal left inverted right x axis y axis)
       3840x2160     60.00 +
    ...
    DP-1-2 connected 3840x2160+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 527mm x 296mm
       3840x2160     60.00*+
    ...
    ```
- only `eDP-1` is used:
    ```
    Screen 0: minimum 320 x 200, current 3840 x 2160, maximum 8192 x 8192
    eDP-1 connected primary 3840x2160+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 346mm x 194mm
       3840x2160     60.00*+
    ...
    DP-1-2 connected (normal left inverted right x axis y axis)
       3840x2160     60.00 +
    ...
    ```
- both screens are outputting something (not my usual workflow, but for completeness)
    ```
    Screen 0: minimum 320 x 200, current 3840 x 2160, maximum 8192 x 8192
    eDP-1 connected primary 3840x2160+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 346mm x 194mm
       3840x2160     60.00*+
    ...
    DP-1-2 connected 3840x2160+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 527mm x 296mm
       3840x2160     60.00*+
    ```

So this function `_get_all_outputs` should return only those outputs that are active now. As you can see, the used outputs have resolution after their name, and also in the list of all resolutions on the next line one of them has `*` symbol next to it. I guess just by looking if there is resolution after `connected` word would be the simplest way.

No, but I can cut the looping short. Right now it will keep trimming sections on \, but realistically it only needs to do that once. The case is where you have dir/part where part is, say, a partial filename. It will first see if "dir/part" is a valid directory, because if it is, you want to list the files in that directory. In this case, where it is not, the first call would fail. It would then see if "dir/" is a directory, which it is, and list the files. Thus you'd get your filename completed. 

However if you tried to complete on a/b/c/foo, if a/b/c isn't a valid directory, there's no value in checking a/b or a/.

If you're willing to trade some memory for lower lock-contention then you can heavily scale back the use of the lock. `list.append()` is atomic, so if you append to a list the total bytes sent then you don't need to lock. And calling `sum()` is also atomic so you could do `sum(sent_chunks)`. And if memory is an issue you could then do a cheap check for when the buffer gets to a certain size and then collapse it. IOW:
```python
self._send progress_buffer = []
...
self._send_progress_buffer.append(length)  # Atomic
if len(self._send_progress_buffer) > 1000:  # Choose some number you're comfortable with.
    with self._progress_lock:
        # In-place replacement to keep non-locking guarantees.
        self._send_progress_buffer[:] = [sum(self._send_progress_buffer)]
# Assignment in this order guarantees that total >= sent if total increases dynamically.
sent = sum(self._send_progress)
total = self._progress_total_bytes
self._progress_callback(sent, total)
```
This example will never lose data, guaranteed (Python unofficial memory model is, "order isn't guaranteed, but you won't lose data"). And if self._progress_total_bytes changes its total as time progresses you at worst might end up slightly out of step with bytes sent versus the total at the time of the send, but as long as the total is dealt with first you shouldn't ever end up with more bytes "sent" than there are in total.

The reasoning for this is that this function returns a mutable dictionary. In principle, one test could inadvertently modify the dictionary, and a second test could pass or fail because of that modification. ``@pytest.fixture`` will call the function and generate a new dictionary each time the data is needed. Adding ``scope="module"`` or ``scope="session"`` instructs ``pytest`` to recreate the data only once per test module (the fixture could be imported into a new module and data would be regenerated) or once per test session (this defeats a large part of the purpose of fixtures).

2. I'd say that the language is a bit better in this article: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_key_certificate (compared to the article you referenced).

3. If I may:
_A public key certificate, or a digital certificate is a combination of a public key and a private key. It is used to be called "SSL certificate". The ultimate practical purpose of a certificate is to enable secure communication between a client program (web browser, e-mail client, etc.) and a server over an encrypted SSL/TLS (transport layer security) connection. The certificate is used to encrypt such a communication and to identify the computer (device)._

And that can be continued:
_The certificate includes information about the key, information about the identity of the computer and the digital signature of the issuer. If the issuer is trusted by the software initiating the communication and the signature is valid, the key can be used to communicate securely with the server identified by the certificate._

---
Note: Since the term "SSL certificate" is still being widely used, I think it would help people making the connection, if they've heard that term.
